title: No edit-schema operation — the schema-write surface offers only create (new) and replace (full-body overwrite); a single-field schema change requires reading the whole schema, reconstructing the full modified body, and passing it back, with no narrow patch primitive
description: The schema-write surface (write-schema Pi tool + scripts/orchestrator/write-schema.ts wrapper, both over schema-write.ts) exposes two operations: create (no prior version exists) and replace (a complete new schema body overwrites the existing file). There is no edit operation that applies a narrow change — add one optional property, widen one enum, adjust one field's description — against an existing schema. Every modification, including adding a single optional field, is forced through full-body replace: read the existing schema, construct the entire modified version in memory, pass the whole document back. The friction differential between this and a direct file Edit is large: a direct Edit changes one line; the canonical replace requires reading the file, rebuilding the full body, and re-passing it via @-file. The friction is load-bearing: it is the mechanism by which schema changes route around the canonical surface. In an unconstrained orchestrator context where a general Edit tool is available against the filesystem, the cheaper direct-Edit path is taken in practice, bypassing the AJV meta-validation, atomic tmp+rename write, and DispatchContext attestation that the canonical surface performs. Two separate models bypassed the canonical surface for schema changes in this project — evidence the friction is not incidental but reliably steers behavior toward the bypass.

evidence.reference: writeSchema / updateSchema / readSchema surface — operations are create + replace; no field-level patch operation
write-schema Pi tool parameters expose operation create|replace; no edit/patch operation
CLI wrapper requires --operation create|replace; full --schema body required for replace

FGAP-136 (the prior schema-write arc) declared 'replace works for non-version-bumping edits (field-shape tweaks that keep the same version value)' — treating replace as the adequate path for field-level edits while not naming the friction that the full-body-replace requirement imposes, which scope-fenced the granularity gap out of that arc

Two models bypassed the canonical schema-write surface via direct file Edit: (1) the in-pi agent / other-model schema change the user referenced; (2) this session's authority-docs surface_kills_mapping field addition done via direct Edit on .context-jit-spec-v2/schemas/authority-docs.schema.json rather than write-schema

impact: Schema modifications route around the canonical schema-write surface in any context where a general file-edit capability is available, losing the AJV meta-validation (schema-is-valid-JSON-Schema check), atomic write, and authorship attestation the canonical surface provides. The bypass is silent: the resulting schema file loads identically whether produced by write-schema or by direct Edit, so nothing downstream objects. The customization-as-tool-driven contract — that substrate authors evolve schemas through the tool surface, no out-of-band edits — holds only when the schema change is large enough that full-body replace is not meaningfully more expensive than the bypass. For the common case (one optional field, one enum value, one description), the bypass is strictly cheaper and is taken.
canonical_vocabulary: edit-schema; schema-write operation; field-level patch; create vs replace vs edit; schema-write surface; canonical schema mutation path
proposed_resolution: Add an edit operation to the schema-write surface (schema-write.ts + write-schema Pi tool + scripts/orchestrator/write-schema.ts) that applies a structured field-level patch to an existing schema without requiring the caller to supply the full body. The patch operates on the schema document via dotted JSON path (mirroring the declarative-transform migration DSL already established in FGAP-136's write-schema-migration: rename / set / delete on dotted paths). Operations the edit primitive supports: add-property (insert a property at a dotted path under properties), remove-property, modify-property (replace a property's subtree), add-enum-value / remove-enum-value (mutate an enum array), set-description, add-required / remove-required (mutate the required array). The handler reads the existing schema, applies the patch, runs the same AJV meta-validation + atomic write + DispatchContext attestation that replace runs, and — when the patch is breaking (remove-property / remove-enum-value / add-required / type change) — refuses unless a version bump + companion write-schema-migration declaration accompanies it, routing the breaking case through the existing FGAP-136 migration machinery. The edit primitive makes the canonical surface cheaper than the direct-Edit bypass for the common single-field case, removing the friction differential that drives the bypass. Auth-gate: edit-schema is a capability-widening substrate-semantics operation; add to AUTH_REQUIRED_TOOLS alongside write-schema + write-schema-migration. Work shape: schema-write.ts gains an editSchema function + patch-op evaluator; write-schema Pi tool gains operation: edit + patch parameter; scripts/orchestrator/write-schema.ts gains --operation edit + --patch @file; test coverage for each patch op + the breaking-change-requires-migration refusal; documentation in the tool description. Plan-mode implementation per the project's source-change discipline.
